sectionDescription: Deep understanding of the rules that govern IFR operations in Australia: CASR, MOS, AIP, minima, RAIM, GNSS, fuel and alternates. Designed for IPC, ATPL-level interviews and airline screening.

categories.title: Regulatory Backbone
categories.description: How CASR, MOS, AIP and company SOPs interact for IFR operations.

modules.title: Regulatory Hierarchy – Who Actually Tells You What To Do
modules.summary: How the Civil Aviation Act, CASR, Manuals of Standards, AIP, Operations Manuals and AFM fit together and which one wins when there is a conflict.

content.text: You are not flying under a single rulebook. IFR operations sit on top of a stack of legal and procedural documents. Airline interviews and IPC examiners love candidates who know where things actually live, not just "I saw it in the AIP somewhere".
The hierarchy – simple version

items: Civil Aviation Act – enables CASA to make regulations.
CASR (Civil Aviation Safety Regulations) – legally binding regulations (e.g. Parts 61, 91, 121, 135, 139).
MOS (Manuals of Standards) – detailed technical rules made under specific CASR Parts (e.g. Part 91 MOS, Part 61 MOS, Part 139 MOS).
AIP (Aeronautical Information Publication) and associated documents – procedures, aeronautical information and operational detail.
Operator's Operations Manual and SOPs – company-level procedures; must comply with CASR and MOS.
Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM) / POH and limitations – legally binding limitations specific to the aircraft type and configuration.

content.text: When two sources appear to disagree, the one higher in the hierarchy wins. Company SOPs or personal habits never override legislation, MOS or AFM limitations.
What examiners and interviewers are looking for

items: You know that MOS is not 'advisory' – it is given legal force by CASR.
You understand that AIP procedures are not optional unless clearly stated.
You know that AFM limitations are hard limits – not suggestions.
You can explain that company SOPs implement, but cannot weaken, regulatory minima.

content.question: In one sentence, explain what the Part 61 MOS does.
content.answer: The Part 61 MOS sets out the detailed standards and requirements for flight crew licences, ratings, endorsements and competency, giving practical effect to the high-level rules in Part 61 of CASR.

content.question: A company SOP seems to allow a lower take-off visibility than the MOS take-off minima. Which one do you follow?
content.answer: You follow the higher standard in law. MOS and CASR requirements override SOPs; a company procedure cannot legally authorise operations below regulatory minima.

content.question: Is an AFM limitation a suggestion or a legal limit? Why?
content.answer: It is a legal limit. The aircraft's certification and continued airworthiness assume compliance with AFM limitations, and operating outside them can breach both airworthiness and operational regulations.

content.question: An interviewer asks: "Where do IFR approach minima actually come from?" Give a concise answer.
content.answer: Approach minima are derived and published through instrument procedure design criteria and are then given operational effect by regulations and MOS that require pilots and operators to comply with those published minima. In practice, they appear in the AIP and charts, but they are enforceable because CASR and the relevant MOS say so.

refs.note: IFR technical detail, minima and equipment requirements.

modules.title: GNSS, RAIM and Integrity – IFR Use
modules.summary: What makes GNSS a legal IFR nav source, what RAIM actually means, when you must advise ATC, and how to handle GNSS failures in enroute and approach phases.

content.text: GNSS is your primary IFR navigation tool, but it only counts as reliable if three things line up: approved installation, valid data, and continuous integrity. RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) is the built-in lie detector that tells you whether the GNSS solution can be trusted for IFR navigation.
Legal conditions for using GNSS under IFR

items: The GNSS unit must be an approved installation and appropriately certified for IFR operations in that aircraft type.
The aircraft documentation (AFM or STC) must explicitly authorise the use of that GNSS installation for IFR navigation and, where applicable, approaches.
The navigation database must be current and valid for the route and procedures being flown; using out-of-date data undermines procedure integrity.
RAIM or an equivalent integrity monitoring function (or SBAS where applicable) must be available for the phase of flight where GNSS is the primary navigation source.

content.text: The role of RAIM – what it actually tells you

items: RAIM is not just "GPS on or off"; it checks whether enough satellites are available with the right geometry to detect a bad signal.
If RAIM is available and not in alarm, you can trust the GNSS position to the required level for that phase of flight.
If RAIM is lost or in alarm, the system can no longer guarantee the integrity of the position solution, even if it still shows you a position.
For approach operations, specific RAIM performance and continuity levels are required; losing RAIM in the final approach segment is a big deal.

content.text: Telling ATC about RAIM loss – the 5-minute rule

In controlled airspace, ATC separation assumptions depend on your stated navigation capability. If RAIM is lost and GNSS integrity is not assured, you are no longer providing the navigation performance they are planning around. The practical rule used operationally is that if RAIM is, or is expected to be, unavailable for more than about 5 minutes while you are using GNSS for navigation in controlled airspace, you must advise ATC and state your remaining navigation capability.

items: RAIM loss or integrity alarm appears while you are navigating using GNSS.
You confirm that this is not a one-second glitch and that integrity really is lost.
If the outage is more than momentary and is likely to persist beyond roughly 5 minutes, you advise ATC.
When advising ATC, you tell them what you still have: for example, "GNSS integrity lost, now navigating with VOR/DME only".
You revert to, or reinforce, alternative navigation sources and review your route and approach options.

content.text: Three RAIM scenarios you must be able to talk through

items: Pre-flight RAIM prediction: RAIM prediction shows integrity will be unavailable during your intended GNSS approach window at destination. You cannot treat that GNSS approach as your only way into the aerodrome. You need another approach type or an alternate.

Enroute RAIM loss in controlled airspace: RAIM is lost, persists beyond a brief blip, and you are using GNSS as primary navigation. You must inform ATC once it is clearly more than transient and state your remaining navigation capability.

Terminal/approach-phase RAIM loss: If RAIM is lost before the final approach fix, you must not commence a GNSS approach. If RAIM is lost after the final approach fix, you must discontinue the approach unless you have another approved way of tracking the procedure safely.

content.text: Database currency and integrity

A GNSS installation is only as good as its data. For IFR, the navigation database must be valid for the AIRAC cycle covering the date of flight. If the database is expired, you cannot treat it as a fully reliable source for instrument procedures unless very specific criteria are met and you verify data against current charts. In many operations, using an expired database for IFR procedures is simply not acceptable.

items: Enroute waypoints and routes: using an expired database could place you on routes that have changed or that no longer exist.
Instrument approaches: obsolete waypoint coordinates or altitudes put you at real terrain and obstacle risk.
Airline and charter SOPs will typically forbid the use of expired databases for IFR procedures entirely.

content.text: Key memory hooks

items: No RAIM, no trust: if RAIM is not available, you cannot treat GNSS as a reliable primary nav source.
Longer than a brief blip + controlled airspace + using GNSS = tell ATC and state your remaining capability.
On GNSS approaches: no RAIM before the final approach fix means no start; RAIM loss after the fix means discontinue unless you have another approved guidance method.
Expired database and IFR don't mix; airlines will not accept "I think it's still close enough" as a defence.

content.question: You are in controlled airspace, navigating on an RNAV route using GNSS. RAIM is lost and the alert persists. How do you explain your actions to an interviewer?
content.answer: I would first confirm the indication is genuine, then revert to my backup navigation sources. If it is clearly more than a brief glitch and will likely persist for more than a few minutes, I would advise ATC that I have lost GNSS integrity and state my remaining navigation capability, such as VOR/DME. I would then review my route and approach options in light of the reduced navigation performance.

content.question: Before departure, RAIM prediction shows RAIM will be unavailable at destination at ETA plus/minus 15 minutes. Your only planned instrument procedure is an RNAV (GNSS) approach. Is that acceptable?
content.answer: No. If RAIM is predicted to be unavailable for the period you plan to use a GNSS approach, you cannot treat that approach as your only way to get in. You must either plan a different type of approach that does not depend on RAIM, adjust your timing to avoid the outage, or plan an alternate that meets the requirements.

content.question: You are established on a GNSS approach and RAIM is lost just after you pass the final approach fix. Weather is at minima. What do you do and how do you justify it?
content.answer: You must discontinue the GNSS approach because the navigation integrity needed for the final segment is no longer assured. Unless you have another approved method of tracking the procedure, the correct action is to go around and either select another type of approach or divert. Continuing below minima in that situation is not defensible.

content.question: An examiner asks: "Why is RAIM loss a big deal if the GPS still shows you a position?" Give a concise, high-level answer.
content.answer: Because without RAIM you have no way of knowing that the position solution is free from undetected errors. IFR procedures and separation standards assume that a certain integrity level is maintained; if RAIM is gone, that assumption is no longer valid, so you cannot rely on the GNSS position for obstacle or separation protection.

content.question: Can you legally continue an RNAV route in IMC purely on dead reckoning if RAIM is lost and there are no ground-based navaids available? Explain your reasoning.
content.answer: Dead reckoning alone does not meet the required standard of navigation performance for IFR enroute in controlled airspace. If RAIM is lost and there is no other approved navigation source, it becomes an abnormal or emergency situation. You should advise ATC, request vectors or assistance, and plan to exit the situation conservatively rather than pretending that dead reckoning alone is a normal substitute.

refs.note: IFR flight and navigation equipment requirements.
Detailed GNSS approval, substitution and integrity requirements.
Operational procedures and restrictions for GNSS approaches.

modules.title: IFR Minimum Heights, LSALT and MSA
modules.summary: How IFR minimum heights are defined, when you can go below them, and why LSALT is a legally serious terrain-protection barrier.

content.text: IFR minimum heights are not rules of thumb. They are the last line of defence between you and terrain. LSALT and MSA exist to guarantee a defined terrain and obstacle clearance margin when you are in cloud or otherwise unable to see and avoid. Busting them without being on a procedure or in a valid exemption window is legally and operationally serious.
What counts as the IFR minimum height for a leg?

items: A published LSALT for the specific route or segment, if one exists.
A published MSA that applies to the sector you are in, when appropriate.
If no applicable published figure exists, your correctly calculated LSALT based on the highest terrain and obstacles plus required clearance margins.

content.text: The regulatory minimum for that segment is effectively the highest of the values that genuinely apply to your route and sector. You cannot cherry-pick the lowest convenient number if a higher one is clearly relevant to the line you are actually flying.
When can you descend below LSALT or MSA?

items: When taking off or landing in accordance with a valid procedure and published minima.
When flying a published instrument approach or missed approach procedure where lower altitudes are explicitly authorised.
When complying with an ATC clearance that itself provides terrain protection, such as radar vectors in an environment where ATC has responsibility for obstacle clearance.
In day VMC, within the bounds of low flying and terrain clearance rules, where you can clearly see and avoid terrain and obstacles.

content.text: The key principle is that you either have procedural protection, surveillance-based protection, or genuine visual terrain awareness. Anything else is just hoping.
Operational mindset

items: Treat LSALT/MSA as a hard floor in IMC, not a flexible guideline.
Know exactly when you are entering and leaving segments of an instrument procedure that authorise lower altitudes.
Be conservative: if in doubt about whether an LSALT applies, assume it does until you are sure otherwise.
Never descend simply because "ATC said" unless you know their clearance sits within a procedure or environment that actually provides terrain clearance.

content.question: You are at LSALT in IMC on an enroute segment. ATC offers you a lower level for traffic. Are you automatically safe and legal to accept?
content.answer: No. ATC clearances do not override your responsibility for terrain clearance unless they are operating in a context where they have explicit responsibility for obstacle clearance (for example, certain radar vector environments). If you cannot be sure that the lower level is terrain-safe, you should decline and explain you need to maintain your minimum safe altitude.

content.question: Your calculated LSALT for a segment is 6,000 ft. The published MSA for the area is 4,500 ft but does not consider a ridge close to your actual track. Which minimum should you use and why?
content.answer: You should treat 6,000 ft as the effective minimum for your route because it properly considers the terrain and obstacles relevant to your actual track. MSA is often a coarse, sector-wide figure and may not capture the worst case for your specific line.

content.question: You are IFR by day in excellent VMC over flat terrain with all obstacles in sight and well below you. Can you descend below the published LSALT and still be legal?
content.answer: Yes, under day VMC the regulations allow operations below LSALT within certain limits, provided you still comply with low flying and terrain clearance rules and maintain safe visual separation from terrain and obstacles. Once you re-enter IMC or lose the ability to see and avoid terrain, you must again be at or above the appropriate IFR minimum height.

content.question: An interviewer asks you to explain LSALT vs MSA in simple terms. How do you answer?
content.answer: MSA is usually a broad-brush minimum altitude for a sector that gives a terrain and obstacle clearance margin within a certain radius of a point, like a navaid. LSALT is calculated specifically for a route or area and gives you the minimum safe altitude for that particular track. In practice, LSALT is more tailored to your route; MSA is more generic.

refs.note: Rules on IFR minimum heights and exceptions.
Detailed requirements for LSALT, MSA and exceptions.
Procedures for deriving and applying minimum altitudes.

modules.title: Approach Ban and IFR Minima
modules.summary: When you are not allowed to start or continue an approach, how decision altitude and MDA really work, and how airline-style thinking treats minima.

content.text: Flying an approach is only half the job. The other half is knowing when you are not allowed to start or continue the approach because the reported conditions, or your aircraft state, fall below what is required. This is the heart of the approach ban concept.
Start vs continue – two distinct decisions

items: Start decision: Are the reported conditions and equipment status good enough to begin the approach at all?
Continue decision: If things deteriorate after you start, at what point are you prohibited from continuing further (for example, approaching the final approach fix)?

content.text: On top of this sits the decision altitude or minimum descent altitude, which is the final gate: you either have the required visual references and a stable profile or you go around. Airline operations treat these gates as hard limits, not vague suggestions.
Basic approach ban logic

items: If the reported visibility or RVR is below the value required for the planned approach minima, you may be prohibited from commencing the approach.
If the visibility drops below the required figure after you commence but before a defined gate (often at or before the final approach fix), you may be prohibited from continuing.
Even if reported visibility is technically above minima, you must go around at DA/MDA if you do not have the required visual references in a stable configuration.

content.text: Required visual reference – more than just a glimpse

items: You must be able to positively identify elements of the runway environment, such as threshold, lights or markings, that allow you to judge your position and flight path.
A brief, intermittent or low-quality glimpse that does not allow a safe, controlled landing does not meet the intent of "visual reference".
Airline SOPs typically add stabilised approach criteria on top of minima: at DA/MDA you must be stable, configured and on profile, with the required visual cues, or you go around.

content.text: Practical airline-style rules of thumb

items: Do not commence an approach if the reported conditions are clearly below published minima unless a specific authorisation exists.
If conditions deteriorate during the approach and breach approach ban criteria before a gate point, go around early and regroup; do not push on hoping it will improve.
At DA/MDA, you either have a stable approach with the required visuals or you execute a missed approach. "I might see it in another second" is not acceptable thinking.

content.question: You are cleared for an ILS with a DA that requires a minimum RVR. When you intercept the localiser the RVR is above the required value, but just before the final approach fix the reported RVR drops below the required minimum. What is the correct action and why?
content.answer: If the applicable approach ban rules state that you cannot continue the approach once RVR drops below the required minimum before a defined gate, you must discontinue the approach and go around or hold until conditions improve. Continuing would violate both the intent of the minima and airline-style conservative decision-making.

content.question: You are at decision altitude on a GNSS approach. You can see a faint glow of runway lights through patchy cloud but do not have a clear, stable view of the runway environment. Are you allowed to continue?
content.answer: No. You must have enough visual reference to identify the runway environment and fly a stable landing path. A faint or intermittent glow that does not let you judge flight path is not enough. The correct action is to go around and either try again or divert.

content.question: How would you explain the approach ban concept in one short, high-level answer to an airline interviewer?
content.answer: The approach ban is the set of rules that stop you starting or continuing an approach when the reported conditions or aircraft status fall below the minima designed to keep the operation safe. It forces early, conservative decisions instead of gambling on marginal weather and late sighting of the runway.

refs.note: Provisions enabling approach ban rules and IFR minima.
Detailed minima and approach ban conditions.
Company implementation of regulatory approach ban policy.

modules.title: Fuel Requirements and Alternates – IFR Decision Logic
modules.summary: How IFR fuel is structured (trip, contingency, alternate, final reserve, holding) and how to think through alternate requirements and isolated aerodromes like an airline pilot.

content.text: Fuel rules exist to stop you from ever being genuinely surprised by fuel state. Airline and charter interviews love fuel questions because they reveal whether you make disciplined, structured decisions or "gut feel" calls.
IFR fuel building blocks

items: Taxi fuel: expected fuel used before take-off.
Trip fuel: from take-off to landing at destination under planned conditions.
Contingency fuel: to cover small variations such as wind error or minor reroutes.
Alternate fuel: to go from destination to alternate if one is required.
Final reserve fuel: typically a set time of holding at a specified altitude (for example, 30 minutes at 1,500 ft) to provide a last-line buffer.
Additional fuel: any extra fuel needed for specific threats, such as forecast holding, expected ATC delay, or engine-out or depressurisation profiles.

content.text: When do you need an alternate?

items: When forecast weather at destination is below or marginally near the required landing minima.
When there are unserviceabilities (such as aids or lighting) that raise the landing minima above forecast weather.
When the aerodrome does not meet requirements for your type of operation (for example, no suitable approach or lighting for night IFR).
When required by specific alternate planning rules in the AIP or MOS (for example, probability tempo/pro conditions, crosswind limits, or other published triggers).

content.text: An airline-style mindset is to treat alternates as a planned risk control, not an admission of defeat. You plan them in calmly on the ground so you are not improvising in the air.
Isolated aerodromes and no-alternate operations

items: Sometimes there is no practical alternate within range; this is an "isolated aerodrome" scenario.
In that case, regulations and MOS set specific extra fuel requirements so you can hold for an extended period and still land with final reserve intact.
You must be able to defend why it was reasonable to treat the aerodrome as isolated and how you met the enhanced fuel requirement.

content.text: Decision-making when fuel gets tight

items: You should be monitoring fuel against plan continuously, not discovering a problem near destination.
If you predict arrival below your planned landing fuel, act early: consider speed, routing, or diverting while options are still good.
Declaring a fuel emergency is not a failure of planning if conditions change beyond what was reasonably planned for; failing to declare when you are close to minimums is a failure.

content.question: You plan an IFR trip with forecast weather at destination just above minima, with tempo conditions that occasionally drop below minima during your ETA window. How does this affect your alternate and fuel planning?
content.answer: The tempo conditions below minima usually trigger an alternate requirement under the applicable rules, so you must plan fuel to carry an alternate. That means you need trip fuel, contingency, alternate fuel and final reserve, plus any additional fuel needed for forecast holding or delays.

content.question: There is no suitable alternate within range of your IFR aircraft. The aerodrome has a reliable history of being open and forecast weather is above minima, but there is still some uncertainty. How do you handle this from a fuel-planning perspective?
content.answer: You are operating into an effectively isolated aerodrome, so you must apply the relevant isolated aerodrome fuel rules, which usually require enough fuel to hold for an extended period (for example, two hours) at a defined altitude before landing with final reserve. You would need to clearly document your assumptions and ensure the required fuel can be carried safely.

content.question: Enroute you realise stronger than forecast headwinds will cause you to arrive at destination with less than planned landing fuel but still above final reserve. How do you explain your decision-making to an examiner?
content.answer: I would first recompute my predicted fuel on board at destination and at my alternate. If continuing to destination leaves me with an uncomfortable margin above final reserve, but diverting early to my alternate gives a much healthier margin, I would likely choose the alternate sooner rather than later. I would communicate with ATC early, update my plan, and treat final reserve as a hard limit, not a goal.

refs.note: Detailed IFR fuel planning requirements and definitions.

modules.title: IFR in Class G vs Controlled Airspace – Responsibilities and Reporting
modules.summary: What changes when you are IFR in Class G vs controlled airspace: separation, terrain responsibility, reporting, and how to talk about it in an interview.

content.text: IFR is not the same experience everywhere. In controlled airspace, ATC owns a lot of the separation problem. In Class G, you are much more on your own, even under IFR. Airline interviewers like to see that you understand where ATC's responsibility stops and yours continues.
IFR in controlled airspace

items: ATC provides separation between IFR flights and, where specified, between IFR and VFR.
You receive clearances for levels, routes and approaches, and are expected to follow them unless you cannot safely comply.
Terrain clearance may be provided by ATC when radar vectors and specific procedures are in use, but you still have ultimate responsibility to query anything that looks unsafe.
Your reporting obligations are defined by the ATC environment and published procedures; missed reports or incorrect readbacks can compromise separation.

content.text: IFR in Class G airspace

items: ATC separation services may not be available; you are responsible for traffic avoidance and terrain clearance within the constraints of IFR.
You must meet reporting requirements to keep ATC and other aircraft aware of your position, especially in non-radar environments.
Standard position reports, including estimates, actuals and tracking information, become critical tools rather than formalities.
IFR in Class G can feel "quiet" but you should assume there are other aircraft out there and act accordingly.

content.question: You are IFR in Class G, non-radar, tracking between two regional aerodromes. What kind of reports and radio discipline are expected from you?
content.answer: I would make the required position reports at the specified points, including estimates and actuals, and broadcast my intentions clearly on the appropriate frequency. I would maintain a good listening watch, use standard phraseology, and provide enough information so that ATC and other aircraft can build an accurate picture of my position and intentions.

content.question: How would you summarise the difference between IFR in Class G and IFR in controlled airspace to an interviewer?
content.answer: In controlled airspace, ATC provides separation and clearances within published rules, and I fit into that system. In Class G, even under IFR, I am carrying much more of the responsibility for traffic avoidance and terrain awareness myself, with ATC providing information and flight following where available rather than full separation.

refs.note: Procedures and reporting requirements for IFR operations in Class G airspace.

modules.title: IFR Recency and IPC – Staying Legally Sharp
modules.summary: How IFR recency, instrument ratings and IPCs fit together under Part 61 and its MOS, and how to talk about your currency in an interview.

content.text: Being able to fly IFR is one thing; being legally allowed to exercise those privileges is another. Airline interviewers will sometimes probe your understanding of recency and IPC rules because it shows whether you treat currency as a box-tick or a professional obligation.
Instrument rating privileges and limitations – big picture

items: Your instrument rating gives you the right to fly under IFR and in IMC in the aircraft categories, classes and types for which you are rated and endorsed.
Those privileges are subject to recency requirements: you must have completed certain instrument flight or simulated instrument tasks in a defined period to remain current.
If your recency lapses, an IPC or other approved means is required to restore full IFR privileges.

content.text: What an IPC actually is

items: An Instrument Proficiency Check is a formal check conducted by an approved examiner to confirm you meet the instrument competency standards.
An IPC typically samples enroute IFR, holding, non-precision and precision or GNSS approaches, and missed approach procedures, plus abnormal scenarios.
A successful IPC can reset certain recency requirements and restore your ability to exercise IFR privileges for the relevant aircraft and operation types.

content.question: An interviewer asks: "How do you stay IFR current and what happens if your recency lapses?" Give a structured answer.
content.answer: I track my instrument flight and approach experience against the recency requirements in the regulations and MOS. That includes a certain number of instrument approaches and flight time in the relevant period, plus any single-pilot IFR requirements where applicable. If my recency lapses, I would not dispatch IFR until I have either completed the necessary recency actions in a simulator or aircraft, or undergone an IPC with an examiner to confirm I meet the required standards and restore my privileges.

content.question: Your IPC is due soon. How would you describe the purpose of the IPC to an examiner in a way that shows maturity?
content.answer: I see the IPC as a structured opportunity to confirm I'm at the standard the system expects for IFR operations, not just a hurdle to clear. It validates both my procedural knowledge and my ability to handle abnormal scenarios, and it's a chance to get professional feedback on my scan, workload management and decision-making under IFR.

refs.note: Instrument rating privileges, recency and IPC provisions.
Detailed standards for instrument rating and IPC content.
